text: It's currently my last day here in Spain before I head to Portugal. So far the trip has been absolutely incredible. I've managed to go to so many different unique markets and try different kinds of food as well as tour the city and learn about all the history that's here in Spain. And on the nature side of things it's been incredible to take hikes up the mountain and see the view from way up there. It's just absolutely breathtaking. Even the coastal towns look completely different than back home. I couldn't help myself so I ended up jumping in the water a little bit, but it was so cold that I quickly decided to get back out and just enjoy the views and relax a bit. And that's really been the theme of this entire trip so far and why I haven't posted too many videos because I'm really trying to just relax and enjoy my time with my family, have some good drinks and some great food. If you guys know that wherever I go I always look for what I can eat and there is a lot to find here in Spain. So I'm going to show you what I found over the last week here. By far the most common edible plants we came across were rosemary and thyme. These grow wild practically along the side of every single trail that we hiked and smell amazing. And even though they're not currently in season it was also pretty common to find some black berry vines. We also came across a lot of fig trees but this was the only one I saw with fruit on it. I also saw quite a few grape vines that had started to send out flowers. And in the city it was pretty common to see bitter orange trees lining the streets that were absolutely beautiful. But I think what surprised me even more was the amount of mulberry trees that were used to line the streets as well, which I thought was an interesting choice because these make a lot of fruit and can really make a mess sometimes. And lastly I came across a couple other fruits that were a little less common including a low-quat tree that was planted next to this gas station and some comequat trees, Kalamundan trees and a pomegranate tree that were planted as decoration at our hotel. Now I just got to Portugal so I'm going to explore and see what I can find over here. But until next time get outside and see what you can find.

It's a beautiful day here in South Florida and today I'm going to go out and see if I can collect everything I need to make dinner tonight. So come with me. First up, we're going to harvest some breadfruit. For those of you unfamiliar with this fruit, it actually acts more like a potato. You can roast, bake, boil, or fry it. But when the fruits are small and green like this one here, my favorite way to prepare them is breadfruit mash, which is kind of like mashed potatoes. After that, I went over to the quail coop and collected some fresh eggs. It's been a couple days since I did this, so there were quite a few. Next, I headed over to my mango tree to pick a couple of them. And it wasn't long before I had a whole bunch. After that, I picked some fresh key limes, as well as some fresh calamans and limes. While the outside of these fruits look pretty similar, the flavor and the color on the inside is completely different. Then I picked some leafy greens from the garden for our salad tonight, including some longevity spinach, some ciso spinach, and cranberry habiscus leaves, which give a nice little cranberry flavor. And last but not least, I harvested some Cuban oregano and rosemary, and now it's time to go find the main course. For the main course, we're going to be targeting an invasive type of freshwater fish here in South Florida, known as the Mayan Cyclad. These fish are aggressive feeders and will eat just about anything, but an earthworm is always a pretty safe bet. In total, today we ended up getting three nice ones. Back at the house, I quickly went ahead and filleted the fish, and now it's time to get to cooking. First, I started by making the breadfruit mash. This is super simple. You just want to remove the skin and the core of the breadfruit, and then boil it until it's tender. Mash it up with a fork, add butter, salt, pepper, and a little bit of chopped up rosemary. Next, I chopped up all our leafy greens from the garden, and made a quick salad dressing using a little calamundan juice, some fish sauce, red chili flakes, and a little bit of ginger. Next, to fry our fish, I went ahead and coated it in flour, then dipped it in an egg wash using the quail eggs we harvested earlier, then coated it with seasoned breadcrumbs, and fried it until a crispy golden brown. And for our dipping sauce, I chopped up our rosemary and oregano, and mixed it with yogurt, a little bit of olive oil, and some salt and pepper. And last but not least, I went ahead and juiced our keylimes to make a delicious key lime pie from scratch, and because I had some extra calamundan juice, I decided to make a calamundan pie as well. Once everything was done, I just plate it it up, and there we have it. Dinner is served. It looks good, and it smells good, but now it's time to see how it tastes. First, I'm going to try a little bit of the salad.
The dressing gives it a nice little sour vinaigrette taste with a nice little kick of spice at the end, but now it's time to try the fish with our sauce.

Mmm, I would put this up against tilapia any day. And breadfruit mash just never gets old. As the fruits get older, they'll get a bit more starchy, and so it'll look more like mashed potatoes, but even like this, they're delicious. And who doesn't love a ripe Julie mango? And to wash it all down, we have our calamundan juice. And now that I'm done with dinner, it's time to try the desserts. First up, I'm going to try the key lime pie out of this world delicious. Time to see how it compares to the calamundan pie. They both are insanely delicious, but I would have to say I'd go with the key lime pie over the calamundan, because you can definitely taste the different flavor profiles, and I just really prefer the key lime. Until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

It's finally time to harvest some jackfruit. If you guys have been following along, you probably remember that I had this huge jackfruit on my tree, but one morning when I went out to harvest it, I realized that someone had come into my yard and taken it right off of the tree. And while that was super disappointing, fast forward to today, and we now have a whole bunch more fruit on this tree and defense to protect them. So today we finally get to harvest them. When jackfruit are ripe and ready to harvest, you'll know it because they get a really strong, fruity smell coming from them. And so when I came out to the tree today, I knew that there was one ready to harvest. And when you give them a nice squeeze, if they have some give to them like this, that's how you know that this is the one that's ready to be picked. But the point where this jackfruit was growing off of the tree was too high for me to reach from the ground. So to pick this one, we have to climb. Now that I'm up here, all we have to do is go ahead and clip it off of the tree and then be careful to bring it down safely because these are pretty heavy.

And just like that, we have our jackfruit. But while I was up there the first time, I realized that there was another fruit that was even more fragrant than the one that we picked already. So I headed back up and eventually I found it. This one right here is borderline overripe, so we're gonna harvest it as well.

Just like that, my workout for the day is complete and we have two nice jackfruit. Most of the time, jackfruit need to be cut in order to remove the fruit segments, but the second one that we harvested was so ripe that I was able to rip it open by just using my hands. As you can see, the inside is filled with different segments of fruit. And what you wanna do is just remove those and then take out the seed in the middle and you're ready to enjoy. Then after removing the seed from the middle of the pod, you can go ahead and pop it right in your mouth.

These are so sweet and have a flavor that's really similar to juicy fruit gum. If you've never tried jackfruit before, I would highly recommend it. Now I'm gonna finish removing the segments so that I can go ahead and put them in the fridge for later. And until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

This fruit right here is known as ice cream bean. Ice cream bean trees are fast growing tropical fruit trees that are pretty rare to see back home in Florida, but here in Puerto Rico, there are quite a few. After making a small cut on the side of the fruit, all you have to do is twist it in order to reveal the fruit that's on the inside. This is the part that you actually eat and it's absolutely delicious. After removing one of the segments of fruit, you want to eat the flesh that surrounds the seed. Now that I have the seed removed, let's give it a try. Mmm, the texture is nice and soft, similar to a marshmallow, and it has a sweet flavor with a little hint of vanilla. There are many different kinds and varieties of ice cream bean out there, but I have to say this one is one of my favorites. I've had a couple different kinds of ice cream beans throughout the years, but this is definitely one of my favorites, so I'm going to be saving the seeds and bringing them back home to my garden to plant. These trees grow fast, and so in just about three to four years, it'll start producing fruit. And the other benefit of having one of these trees in your garden is that they actually fix the nitrogen within the soil, so they're really good for your other trees. So hopefully in a couple years, I'll have my own giant ice cream beans in my garden. I'll definitely be sure to make updates as these start to grow in the garden. And until next time, get outside and see what you can find.
One of the better, the better, the more creamy, you're my body, you know what I'm like? One of the better, the better, the more creamy, you're my body, you know what I'm like?

Today I'm going to take some breadfruit from the garden and make crispy breadfruit hash brown sticks. So come with me. Breadfruit is a tropical fruit tree that produces unique, starchy green fruits that can be used in many of the same ways that you would use a potato. You can bake, boil, fry, or roast them and you know that they're ready to harvest when they start to have some sap dripping down the fruit. When they're ready to harvest you just want to go ahead and twist them around and give a little tug and they should fall right off. I always have to make sure that I harvest these in time otherwise the squirrels will get to them first. Back in the kitchen the first thing I like to do is cut a little hole where the stem used to be. Then I flip it over and score the bottom of it to form an X. Once it looks like this you know that you're ready to add it to an open flame and start to roast it. As it roasts I like to just carefully rotate it that way every side manages to get a nice char to it. Once it's done it should look something like this and the X should have started to split apart. After letting it cool for a little bit I slice it in half then I cut them into quarters that way it's easier to remove each little bit of the core. After that's done you could just remove the skin and enjoy roasted breadfruit or what I personally prefer is that in salt, pepper and butter mashing that up real good flattening that mixture and then slicing thin strips that you can fry until a crispy golden brown. The end result are crispy breadfruit sticks that are very similar to hash browns in perfect for breakfast. These are absolutely delicious one of my favorite things to do is just dip them in the egg yolk and then go ahead and take a bite. Let me know if you'd give these a try and until next time get outside and see what you can find.

This fruit tastes like a mixture of banana, mango, and pineapple. It's known as monstera delicioso fruit and comes from a popular house plant often referred to as the Swiss cheese plant, but not many people realize that it actually produces edible fruit. You know these fruits are ready to pick when they're nice and full in size and the scales have started to slightly spread apart. Then you just wanna set them cut side up into a glass and wait until the scales start to loosen up. When the fruit is ripe and ready to be enjoyed, you should be able to lightly tap on it or flick them with your finger and they should fall right off. It's really important to let these fruits ripen on their own and not to pry off the scales because if you eat the unripe fruit, it can actually burn your mouth. Underneath the scales, you'll see that the ripe fruit actually comes apart in individual segments that perfectly line up with the scales. It is a really unique fruit and the aroma of it smells just like a fragrant pineapple. And the flavor in my opinion is like you combine banana, mango, and pineapple all in one fruit.

It's really good. Unlike a lot of other fruits, these tend to ripen in sections rather than the entire fruit ripening at once. So each day as the scales fall off, you get to enjoy a little bit more of it. Let me know down in the comments if you'd give this fruit a try. And until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

It's been a little while since my last running video, so today I'm going to go for a run and see what fruits in season, so come with me. A lot of people have been asking how I can get such a good time running while filming all these fruits, and the truth is, for the first couple miles, I just jog or walk. After the warm-up, that's when I try to get my best mile time. The first thing I ended up coming across was the star fruit tree that had started to send out a whole bunch of flower and set a couple little fruit. After that, I came across these two citrus trees that were loaded with fruit, and at first I thought they were grapefruit, but they look a little too big, so I think these are actually pomello. After that, I came across this papaya tree that had sprouted up right on the outside of this fence, and was loaded with fruit, as well as a butterfly pea flower vine growing right beside it. After that, I came across some dragon fruit, also known as pataya, that had climbed all the way up this tree, and then started making fruit. Next, I came across some bananas, as well as some monstera deliciosa fruit, that still have a ways to go before they'll be ripe and ready to pick. And I'm not sure how well you can see this, but this right here is actually a guava tree, and it has a fruit way up at the top of it. I also came across a smaller citrus tree that was loaded, as well. I'm not sure exactly if these are lemons or limes, but still pretty cool to see them. And last but not least, you know it wouldn't be Florida if you didn't see some coconuts. And now that the warm up's done, it's time for the run! I ended up getting my new best mile time of 7 minutes and 18 seconds, which is 22 seconds faster than my previous. I am now officially exhausted. Now I'm gonna go take a much needed shower, and until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

Today I'm craving sushi, so let's head out on the boat and see what we can find to make a delicious dinner. As soon as I jumped in the water, I loaded up my speargun and then it was time to go search for dinner. This first spot is about 65 feet of water, so today I'm going to be using a throw flasher, which is just a PVC pipe covered with reflective tape that looks like a struggling fish floating down to the bottom. Personally, I like to keep an eye on the throw flasher, and once it starts to get close to the bottom and I see some fish checking it out, I take a deep breath and head down to see if any of those fish are good ones to bring home for dinner. One of my favorite fish to bring home for sushi here in South Florida is called the Sero macro, and this one came in to check out the flasher so I was careful to go down slowly in order not to scare it and then hopefully line up and take the perfect shot. These fish make great sushi because not only are they delicious, but they also are a soft-bodied fish that just melts in your mouth, but when spearfishing for them you have to take a really good shot, otherwise they'll pull off the spear. Even though it wasn't perfect, luckily my shot managed to hold, so just like that, we have our first part of dinner secured. After adding the macro to the cooler, I decided to dive a little bit more shallow, so we headed into about 15 to 20 feet of water where I came across some Florida spiny lobsters, so I went ahead and grabbed my lobster lasso, placed it around its tail, and then pulled it tightly closed, and just like that we have our first lobster of the day. Here in Florida you're allowed six lobsters per person per day as long as they're big enough, so I went ahead and repeated the same process until we had enough for dinner. Back at the dock I quickly went ahead and filleted the macro and then cleaned all of the lobsters, and now we're ready to head back inside and start to make dinner. Tonight I'm going to be making tempura lobster sushi rolls topped with sarrow macro. First I started by cutting down the bottom side of each one of the lobster tails and removing all the meat from the inside. After that I went ahead and tried to cut the lobster tails so that they would be the perfect size to fit right inside of our sushi rolls. After that I made a quick and easy tempura batter, went ahead and lightly coated the lobster with some flour dipped it into the batter and then into some hot oil it went until it was a nice crispy golden brown. While I waited for that to cook I also boiled the shells because I decided that they would look pretty cool for the presentation tonight. Once the lobster was done cooking I cut it into strips so that it would be easier to add to the sushi roll and then added avocado and asparagus rolled it up tight and gave it a nice press with the bamboo mat. After that was done I topped it with some fresh sarrow macro and then went ahead and sliced it up and there we have it our delicious sushi roll and just like that a little while later dinner is served. It really doesn't get any more fresh than that. Now I'm going to enjoy my dinner and until next time get outside and see what you can find.

Today when I was out diving for stone crabs I came across one of my favorite animals ever the day started off pretty normal I just went out on the boat hopped in the water and started looking for some stone crabs And it wasn't long before I came across them to get these out of the hole I just like to quickly reach my hand in and then scare them out and just like that we have our stone crab The trickiest part when you're diving for stone crabs is being able to quickly grab them without them grabbing you Because although their claws are delicious they pack a punch and if they grab a hold of your finger You're gonna feel it. But here's where the day started to get really interesting as I was diving this spot I kept coming across old stone crab skeletons Which means that something here has also been eating these stone crabs I had a little bit of a suspicion that I knew which animal was behind the stone crab skeletons because not many things Target these big powerful crabs But it wasn't until I stuck my hand in this hole looking for another stone crab that my suspicions were confirmed When I stuck my hand in here there wasn't a stone crab But there was this big octopus These are one of my all-time favorite animals to find when I'm out diving But they're amazing at camouflage so it's rare that you actually get to see one But when I stuck my hand into the hole it turns out that this guy was in there instead of the stone crab So it looks like we're both looking for the same meal A lot of times if you swim beside these and just place your hand out gently once they realize that you're not a threat They'll stop inking which is their defense mechanism and they'll eventually just rest on your hand So I was trying to do that so I could place him back by the hole that I found him But he was having no part of it So I just decided that I would let him go on his way and figure out where he would go to next and just keep a little eye on him and let him have his space It is so cool to watch these crawl along the bottom because in a split second they can change their entire body's color right before your eyes And if changing colors right before your eyes isn't cool enough Check this out every single step that it takes it's sticking those tentacles into each one of the tiny holes Trying to figure out if there's a new hiding spot that it can disappear into

It really is incredible how they have the ability to disappear right before your eyes as it was walking along here I thought that I'd be able to follow it for quite some time because I didn't think that there were any holes nearby But this tiny little space right here that it ended up finding was just enough that it could completely Disappear and before you know it the entire octopus is underground and you would never know that it was ever there

This is just another perfect example of why it's so incredible to find these on your dive because if I wasn't following this one as closely as I was I would have absolutely no idea that that big octopus was hiding inside of this tiny little hole right here

In my opinion when it comes to camouflage the octopus has a talent that just simply is unmatched If someone else were to be swimming by right now they would have no idea that it's even there get outside and see what you can find

Today I was craving stone crab claws, but before I dig in and enjoy these, let me show you how we got them. I went out in the boat, jumped in the water, and it was time to find some stone crabs. The first thing I came across on my dive was this coral head that was loaded with tons of small floor to spiny lobsters. While these aren't big enough to take home to eat, it is a good sign because you know that their population is growing. A little while after that, I came across a good sign. This right here isn't a dead stone crab, it's actually a stone crab mole. Because as they grow, they end up shedding their exoskeleton. Both lobsters and stone crabs do this, so it's common to see them if they're in the area. And it wasn't long before I came across the first stone crab of the day. When diving for stone crabs, the trick is to manage to reach in the hole quickly and get the crab out and manage to secure it before it manages to grab onto you. Because although the claws are delicious, they definitely pack a punch and you do not want these guys managing to pinch onto one of your fingers. It really hurts. After catching the stone crab, the next thing I do is I go ahead and measure the claw and make sure that it's big enough to keep. Here in the state of Florida, you don't actually keep the entire stone crab like a lot of other crab species. Instead, you actually just harvest the claws and then you go ahead and release the crab and it'll regrow its claw another day. To remove the claw, rather than forcefully breaking it off which can cause a life-threatening injury to the crab, I like to poke the membrane in its arm right here, which actually causes it to release it itself, which is one of its natural defense mechanisms. Then I just keep repeating the same process of catching the crabs, removing the claws, and then bringing them back down and letting them go in the exact same holes that I found them so that they can regrow them another day. Today, even came across a stone crab that was actually in the process of re-growing one of its claws. As you can see, it has one claw that looks at normal proportionate size, but then if you look on the other side, there's a teeny tiny new claw that's starting to form. And this is how you're able to have sustainable stone crabs for generations to come. But even though it's illegal for people to harvest the whole crab, there are other animals that definitely enjoy stone crab as well. If this octopus I found while diving had its way, it definitely would be eating a lot more than just a claw. Back inside, I brought some water to a boil and just added our stone crab claws in for the next five minutes. And after the five minutes was up, I just quickly removed the claws and added them to an ice bath. While I waited for those to chill, I just whipped up a classic stone crab mustard dipping sauce combining powdered mustard and mayonnaise, and then mixing in all the rest of the ingredients. And I actually find that the flavor of the sauce is even better if you let it sit in the fridge for a couple hours. Then when I'm ready to serve, I just go ahead and crack the crab claws. And just like that, we have delicious, fresh stone crab claws making the perfect appetizer. Stone crab season is something that I look forward to every single year. You just give it a nice dunk in the mustard sauce and enjoy.

Mmm, does not get any more fresh than that. It's crazy to think that this same stone crab that we're enjoying right now is also alive and well in the ocean, and we might very well enjoy it in a couple years again. Now I'm going to finish eating the rest of these and until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

Sprouted coconuts are one of my favorite snacks, so today we're gonna crack one open and try it. The best place to find a sprouted coconut is at the base of a mature coconut palm that has lots of fallen older coconuts. It can take up to six months for them to sprout, but with the right conditions it'll eventually happen, and the best tasting ones are the ones that have just started to sprout. Back at the house I removed the outer husk, and now it's time to crack it open. The best way to do this is by hitting it with the back end of a big knife. You don't want to use the sharp end otherwise you're gonna dull your knife, and be sure to do this over a bowl because sometimes there's leftover coconut water. When you think of a coconut you might think of it being hollow, but once it sprouts it fills with a spongy inside commonly referred to as a coconut apple. These are so different from the coconuts that you'll see for sale at the grocery store. Not only do they have the nice airy texture that just melts in your mouth, but they have just a hint of coconut with a sweet flavor that's just delicious. And the insides of these also have lots of coconut oil. So if you ever get the chance to try a sprouted coconut I would highly recommend it. And get outside and see what you can find.

This plant right behind me is actually where sugar comes from, so today we're gonna harvest some. This plant is known as sugar cane and is the source of over 75% of the world sugar. So today we're gonna harvest some of these stocks from the garden and make our own homemade sugar. The first thing I did was go ahead and make a cut down at the base to harvest three of the largest sugar cane stocks. Next after harvesting the sugar cane stocks that we're gonna use today, I just went ahead and removed the top leaves. That way all that we're left with are the stocks. Next I just cut them into manageable sized pieces and we're gonna remove the skin. To do this you can either use a vegetable peeler like I am here or a knife. After removing the skin of the sugar cane it should look something like this. Then you want to carefully stand it upright and use your knife to go ahead and split the stock in half. Then you just want to cut each one of the stock halves into small pieces. That way they're easy to blend and they don't overwork the blender. After repeating the same process for the rest of the sugar cane this is what we're left with. The skins we can go ahead and compost and then some of the pieces that have some roots on them. I'm gonna end up saving because all you have to do to replant this is stick it sideways like this into the ground and each one of these little nodes that are on the sides will end up sprouting up and becoming new sugar cane. The sugar cane is absolutely delicious on its own so at this point you could just go ahead and pop it right in your mouth. You don't actually eat the fibers of the sugar cane though you just suck out all the juice and in order to make the sugar we need to do that just on a much larger scale. To juice the sugar cane I like to just go ahead and blend it but you want to add a little bit of water that way you don't overheat the blender and make sure that the pieces are small enough that it manages to chop it thoroughly. After blending up all the sugar cane I just added it into a bowl lined with some cheesecloth and we're just gonna go ahead and squeeze out all the remaining juice. After you're done removing all the juice this is what it should look like. The sugar cane juice is delicious on its own but to make granular sugar we have a few more steps. You want to add your sugar cane juice to a large pan on medium heat and let it simmer. As it starts to simmer you'll notice that a green foam starts to come to the surface and you just want to try to skim off as much of it as you possibly can. Then you just want to continue to let the mixture cook down and let all of the water evaporate that way you're left with the condensed sugars. Once you get to the point that you stir it and it leaves behind a trail you know that you're close to it being done but to double check that it's ready you can add a drop to some cold water and if it forms a soft sugar ball then you know that it's good. At this point you want to quickly remove the pan from the heat that way you make sure that the cooking process stops and you don't end up burning it and then you just want to stir this mixture until it turns into sugar. I went ahead and sped this part up for you guys because it definitely takes quite a while but eventually if you keep stirring the mixture it becomes more thick and even more thick until it finally becomes a little bit of a sandy gritty texture and that's how you know that you have your sugar made. Then I added the mixture to a cookie sheet lined with some parchment paper and let the excess moisture dry for about two hours before crushing it and sifting it and just like that we have our homemade cane sugar. The process is definitely a bit time consuming but you're left with natural pure sugar that's absolutely delicious.
So let me know if you'd give this a try and until next time get outside and see what you can find.

This tree right behind me is actually where cinnamon comes from, so today we're gonna harvest some. Over the past year, so this tree has grown a lot, so it's time to trim it. After trimming the tree, I was left with quite a few good-sized branches. The cinnamon that we know and love is actually just the dried out inner bark from the cinnamon tree, so if you look closely, you can actually see that there's a dark ring around the branch. To get to that inner bark, the first thing you want to do is go ahead and use a vegetable peeler to remove the outer bark. After that, if I'm using a larger log like this one right here that has a branch coming off the side, I like to make a cut all the way around it, that way I know where to stop for my cinnamon stick, and then I just make a cut from the top to the bottom. After that, I like to give it a good tap all the way around because this helps to loosen that inner bark. After that's done, I go back to that center line that I cut earlier and carefully use my knife to peel off the inner bark layer. The larger the branches, the easier it is to get it all off in one piece. Then I just go ahead and lay all the bark out on a cookie sheet so that it can dry overnight. The next morning, after it's dried out, you can see that it curls up and becomes the cinnamon sticks that we know and love. Then for any of the smaller or broken cinnamon sticks, what I like to do is roughly chop them that way they fit inside of a coffee grinder and then grind them to make ground cinnamon. Once that's done, it should look something like this. Then you can go ahead and sift it and just like that, we have cinnamon sticks and ground cinnamon straight from the garden. So if you ever wondered how cinnamon's made, now you know. Until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

In light of the holidays, I decided to make cinnamon sugar cookies with toasted coconut. So let me show you how I made these. Mmm. First things first, we've got to go get a coconut. Luckily, this tree right here had quite a few. So I just carefully climbed up and then went ahead and picked one of them. Then back at the house, I went ahead and just removed the husk of the coconut and then drained out all the water. That way, we could crack it open and get to the meat on the inside. There are quite a few ways that you can remove the coconut meat from the shell. But for me, I find that the easiest way is to just slide my knife right in between the coconut meat and the shell and make a cut all the way around until the entire thing pops out. After moving all the coconut meat from the shell, I went ahead and added it to my blender and gave it a small pulse to shred it into small pieces before adding it into the oven to toast. After it was finished toasting, this is what we're left with. Along with this toasted coconut, I'm also going to be using some homemade sugar that I made from juicing some of my sugar cane, cooking it down, and creating my own homemade cane sugar. And the cinnamon is also going to come from my garden because earlier when I trimmed this tree, I didn't want the branches to go to waste. So I ended up making my own cinnamon sticks and some ground cinnamon. To make the cookies, I started by adding some of our homemade ground cinnamon and our toasted coconut to the rest of our dry cookie ingredients. Then we can go ahead and give this a good stir and set it to the side while we work on the rest of our wet ingredients. In a separate bowl, I combined two tablespoons of butter with half a cup of our finely sifted homemade cane sugar. Once that was nice and mixed, I just added in an egg yolk and combined all of it together. After the wet ingredients were thoroughly combined, then I just started adding a little bit of our dry mixture at a time and mixing it in until we had the entire cookie dough made. The consistency that you're looking for is something just like this. Today I'm craving lobster and stone crab, but I'm not about to go buy some when I can go catch my very own. So let me show you how we got these. Then I just kept repeating the same process of catching the crabs removing the large claws and letting them go so that they can regrow them another day and there were quite a few of them today that had some giant claws and while I was doing this I ended up being greeted by a very tiny little nurse shark. It's passion fruit season here in South Florida, so let's go pick some. My friend Zane over at Zane's World Nursery has one of these passion fruit vines that's absolutely enormous and covers this entire section. And there are so many fruits. Because passion fruit have a tough outer skin, you can wait until they drop off of the vine and then just go around and collect them all. It wasn't long before I had a whole little bag full of them. Back in the kitchen, I gave the passion fruit to quick wash, cut them in half, and then used a spoon to scoop out all the delicious seeds on the inside. And because we have so much passion fruit to work with, today I decided to make passion fruit curd. For anyone that's never tried this before, passion fruit curd can be used similar to the way you use jelly. You can just top it on toast and it's absolutely delicious. And it's super simple to make. All you want to do is add half a cup of your passion fruit, a pinch of salt, three quarters of a cup of white sugar, and three eggs to a bowl and give that a good mix. You want to make sure that the bowl you're using is heat resistant or just use a smaller pot because you want to add this to a larger pot with some simmering water to create a double boiler effect. And then just start for the next eight minutes until it starts to thicken. After it gets to a nice thicker consistency, I just like to remove it from the heat and then add one whole stick of butter, one little cube at a time, and mix it until it all melts and combines together. After it's all mixed together, it should look something like this. And then I just like to add it to jars so it can finish thickening and chill in the fridge. And just like that, you have homemade passion fruit curd that makes it delicious and easy breakfast in the morning. How it's time to see how it tastes.

It's the perfect blend of sweet and sour and I'll definitely be enjoying this for breakfast this week. One of the things that Guatemala is well known for is their amazing coffee, so today I'm at a coffee farm and we're going to take a tour. Located on the outskirts of Antigua, Guatemala lies a hidden gem, Finka la Azotea. They offer daily coffee tours here, not only teaching about the history of coffee and how it became loved worldwide, but also the process in which it goes from their farm to your cup. After learning a bit about the history behind the coffee plants and how they became loved worldwide, then you get to head out into their very own farm and see all the coffee plants in person for yourself. And at this point in the tour, they also let everyone pick one of the coffee cherries so that you can taste it for yourself. When the coffee cherries are nice and dark red like this, they're sweet on the inside and when you spit out the seeds, those are the coffee beans that we know and love. After that, you'll walk through the rest of the tour learning how they ferment the coffee beans, then drive them out in the sun, remove the parchment layer, and finally roast them to be the perfect light, medium, or dark roast coffee that you know and love. And then after you've learned about the hard work and process that goes into making this incredible coffee, then you finally get to try it.
So if you visit Antigua Guatemala, I would highly recommend booking a coffee tour with Finka de Asotea. And until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

Today's a big day, we're transplanting the soursoft tree to the new garden. A couple weeks ago, I started by root pruning this entire tree to make sure that it could grow some new roots closer in so that it would have a better chance of success when we go to transplant. And today's the big day, it's finally time to dig this thing up and move it to the new house. Fortunately, the roots on soursoft trees are pretty shallow, so it's easy to get underneath them and cut them free in order to be able to transplant the tree. Moving any tree this size is exhausting and a whole lot of work, but these typically move a lot easier than some other tropical fruit trees because they don't have very big roots. After I had the root ball free, I pruned the tree that way it would have less of a canopy to support while it transitions to its new home. Then I bagged up the roots and it's time to load this into the truck and take it to the new house. On the way there, I also covered it with a tarp that way it would protect it from any wind damage as well as sun damage on the drive. Back at my house, I dropped it into the ground, backfilled it with soil, and now I'm just giving it a nice deep watering and I'm going to continue to do this for the next couple weeks until it settles into its new home. To minimize the stress from transplanting, as well as the fact that it already has dropped a lot of leaves due to the cold snaps, I did give it some shade coverage and some wind protection. And to lock in extra moisture and nutrients, I added oak leaves around it, that way it would really be able to hold in the moisture. It'll be a couple weeks before we know for sure if this worked or not, but I'm pretty confident that it'll bounce back and be better than ever, but we'll just have to wait and see. Until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

This week we're projected to get some of the coldest temperatures that South Florida has seen in the past 15 years and this tree right behind me is not going to enjoy that. As you can see it's already lost a whole bunch of its leaves from the earlier cold snaps and if it actually gets down to 32 it could possibly kill this tree. But I don't give up so easily so it's time to get creative. If you saw my previous video you know that I already built some wind and sun protection to try to help this tree establish at its new home but that's not going to be enough to keep the cold out. So what I've started doing right here is I built this PVC frame over the top of the tree and we're going to cover the entire tree to try to preserve the heat in the inside. Underneath each one of these PVC pipes is a piece of rebar that keeps it anchored into the ground and then a cross section of PVC pipe to keep it connected to each one of the other sides. Next thing that I did was cover each one of the sides of the frame with plastic wrap. This will create a greenhouse-like effect and keep the wind out as well as the heat on the inside. The problem with the plastic though is it doesn't allow for ventilation and if it touches any of the branches it'll make them even colder because it holds onto that cold. So over the top of our frame I'm going to be adding this frost cloth that'll not only help keep the heat in and the cold out but it also allows for ventilation and it's safe for any of the branches that might bump into it. Think of this like putting on a jacket to help keep the cold out and the warm on the inside except for the fact that trees don't produce their own heat. So in order to keep heat on the inside you have to add some. So to add some warmth here on the inside I've added incandescent string lights to the base of the trunk which will help to add heat to it and the roots and as the heat builds it'll rise up to the rest of the canopy. Now that it's fully covered this is what it looks like. It doesn't look that pretty but I hope that it will be effective. When I look on in the inside here I can already feel that it's much warmer here. During the days I can raise this side so that there's nice ventilation for the tree and then at night when it gets cold I can shut it back down. Now I'm just going to deeply water the tree and we'll just have to see how this next week goes. It's already starting to get warm in here so I'm pretty hopeful that this will work but we just have to wait and see. Until next time get outside and see what you can find.
The first round of mulch has finally been delivered, so it's time to mulch the garden today. But before I spread all this out, I have to try something. It just looks too perfect.

You know that it's cold here in South Florida when it starts raining iguanas. It is currently so cold here in South Florida that iguanas like this one are falling out of the trees. So today, we're making iguana tacos. Green iguanas are an invasive species here in South Florida that compete with our native wildlife for resources. But because they're not from here, they aren't adapted to the cold. So when we get these cold fronts, they become too cold and they fall straight out of the trees. Whenever we have these cold fronts and they start falling down, a lot of people will go out and collect them to help control the number in the population. Once it was done, I topped it with black pepper and lime and we have our sauce. I like to let the edges crisp up until they look something like this. In the leftover oil, I crisp up some tortillas. Then I added in the iguana meat, topped it with some pickled red onions, jalapeno and cilantro and then drizzled on our sauce. And there we have it, iguana tacos. This past week I made a guana tacos and it really shook the internet and I got a whole bunch of hate so let's talk about ethical eating. Now I totally get if you just won't eat a guana taco everybody has their limits that's fine. But overall that wasn't the main issue that people had in the comments so let's talk about it. Now when I made the original video I said that a guana's are an invasive species here in South Florida but just in case anyone's not sure of the criteria this is it specifically here in South Florida this is the impact that a guana's have on our local ecosystem. Due to the extreme cold temperatures we had this week that immobilized a lot of them in Florida fish and wildlife had drop off points and collected over 5,000 of them most of which ended up being euthanized. So rather than let the one that I found go to waste I decided to make tacos and I would argue that it's the most ethical taco you could possibly have. Locally hunting and sourcing your own food in general saves the entire planet from greenhouse gases of your food having to travel around the world to get to your plate. When it comes to invasive species then you're not only helping the entire planet by not flying it across the world but you're also helping your local ecosystem. That brings us to point number two. You all do realize that chicken and beef and all of that comes from somewhere right? It comes from farms and personally after seeing the conditions that many of these animals are in at these farms I think that these have it way better off. They have a great life and then one bad day rather than some of the animals on these farms having an awful life. And that brings us to point number three. Why don't you just go vegan? And personally I don't have any beef with vegans but I also will say that I do think that this is also better than even going vegan and here's why. A lot of our produce, fruits and vegetables as well as vegan products come from monocultured crops and those have to grow somewhere which means that areas for wildlife habitat have to be bulldozed in order to do so. And then on top of that they have to fly over to get to your table. Hopefully this explains the why behind my iguana tacos and challenges people to think about where their food comes from. Until next time get outside and see what you can find.
